pages.title: Welcome to Ransomware & Service Disruption Awareness
pages.subtitle: Learn how ransomware can disrupt public services, why reporting and isolation matter, and how local government employees can reduce harm.

video.caption: Introduction to ransomware and service disruption for local government

pages.title: Why ransomware hits governments hard
pages.text:
Ransomware is a type of malware that can lock systems, encrypt files, and disrupt normal operations while attackers demand payment.

For local governments, that does not just mean computer trouble. It can delay payroll, utility billing, records access, permits, court functions, police workflows, and other public services.

When services stop working, the impact is felt by both employees and the public.

pages.title: Close to home: the Golf Manor example
pages.text:
Ransomware is not just a big-city or federal problem. In Ohio, the Village of Golf Manor reported a ransomware incident that disrupted systems and raised questions about whether officials should pay to restore access.

Later coverage said the village considered a larger ransom demand and that council approved payment of $2,000. The case also drew attention because Ohio law now restricts when a political subdivision may comply with a ransom demand.

Ohio Revised Code 9.64 says a political subdivision experiencing a ransomware incident shall not pay or otherwise comply unless its legislative authority formally approves the payment or compliance in a resolution or ordinance explaining why it is in the political subdivision's best interest.

A ransomware incident can put a local government under pressure very quickly, especially when systems and services are already disrupted.

References: DataBreaches coverage | Ohio Revised Code 9.64

pages.title: How ransomware often starts
pages.text:
Ransomware often begins with something that seems small at first.

Common entry points include:

  • Phishing emails and malicious attachments.
  • Compromised accounts or weak passwords.
  • Remote access abuse.
  • Malicious downloads or unsafe links.
  • Unpatched systems or exposed services.

Employees may not always see the technical side, but they can still notice warning signs early.

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text: Ransomware is not only a technical problem. It can become a service continuity problem very quickly.

bullets.title: Backups can be affected too
bullets.text: If backups are poorly protected or too connected to the main environment, attackers may encrypt or destroy them along with production systems.

bullets.title: Service disruption is a public issue
bullets.text: Residents may lose access to government services even if the attack only started on a few internal systems.

bullets.title: Fast reporting reduces harm
bullets.text: The sooner suspicious activity is escalated, the better the chance of containing it before it spreads.

bullets.title: Ransom payment is not a simple solution
bullets.text: Federal guidance warns that paying does not guarantee recovery and may encourage future attacks.

pages.title: What employees may notice first
pages.text:
Early warning signs do not always look dramatic.

Employees may notice:

  • Files that suddenly cannot be opened.
  • Shared drives becoming unavailable.
  • Strange file extensions or ransom notes.
  • Systems slowing down or locking users out.
  • Unexpected account activity or security alerts.
  • Multiple departments losing access at the same time.

Do not assume someone else has already reported it.

pages.title: What to do first
pages.text:
If ransomware is suspected:

  1. Stop interacting with the affected system.
  2. Report the incident immediately through your agency's process.
  3. Do not delete files, wipe devices, or try to fix it quietly.
  4. If directed by your agency, disconnect affected devices from the network.
  5. Follow response instructions from IT, leadership, and incident responders.

Quick reporting and isolation can reduce spread and protect other systems.

image.alt: Immediate ransomware response flow for local government (replace later)

pages.title: Reporting and outside support
pages.text:
Ransomware incidents often involve both internal response and outside reporting.

Agencies may need to coordinate with:

  • OCIC / Ohio Homeland Security for Ohio reporting and coordination.
  • Auditor of State for the required reporting process.
  • FBI and IC3 for cybercrime reporting and investigative support.
  • CISA and the Joint Ransomware Task Force for broader ransomware coordination and guidance.

Ohio law requires political subdivisions to notify Ohio Homeland Security as soon as possible but no later than 7 days after discovery, and the Auditor of State no later than 30 days after discovery.

Helpful links: OCIC | IC3 | FBI Tips | Joint Ransomware Task Force

pages.title: Why backups and continuity matter
pages.text:
Good backups are important, but they are not enough by themselves.

Backups help only if they are:

  • Regularly maintained.
  • Tested to confirm recovery works.
  • Protected from unauthorized access.
  • Separated well enough that attackers cannot easily encrypt them too.

Government organizations also need continuity planning so critical services can keep functioning during recovery.

pages.title: What employees should remember
pages.text:
Ransomware response is not just a technical issue. It is about protecting operations, services, and public trust.

Remember:

  • Report early.
  • Do not hide the problem.
  • Do not destroy evidence.
  • Follow agency response procedures.
  • Support continuity and recovery efforts.

Fast action can reduce damage across the whole organization.

pages.title: Ready for the quiz?
pages.subtitle: This quiz will test your understanding of ransomware, service disruption, and response steps for local government.
